content:
# Leviticus Chapter 10 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 63r–63v | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus pp. 94–95

---

**1** Now Nadab and Abihu, the sons of Aaron, each took his censer and put fire in it and laid incense on it and offered unauthorized fire before the LORD, which he had not commanded them.

**2** And fire came out from before the LORD and consumed them, and they died before the LORD.

**3** Then Moses said to Aaron, "This is what the LORD has spoken: 'Among those who are near me I will be sanctified, and before all the people I will be glorified.'" And Aaron was silent.

**4** And Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan, the sons of Uzziel the uncle of Aaron, and said to them, "Come near; carry your brothers away from the front of the sanctuary to a place outside the camp."

**5** So they came near and carried them in their tunics outside the camp, as Moses had said.

**6** And Moses said to Aaron and to Eleazar and Ithamar his sons, "Do not let your hair hang loose, and do not tear your garments, lest you die, and wrath come upon all the congregation. But your brothers, the whole house of Israel, may weep over the burning that the LORD has kindled.

**7** And you shall not go out from the entrance of the tent of meeting, lest you die, for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you." And they did according to the word of Moses.

**8** And the LORD spoke to Aaron, saying,

**9** "Drink no wine or strong drink, you or your sons with you, when you go into the tent of meeting, lest you die. It shall be a statute forever throughout your generations.

**10** You are to distinguish between the holy and the common, and between the unclean and the clean,

**11** and you are to teach the people of Israel all the statutes that the LORD has spoken to them by Moses."

**12** Moses spoke to Aaron and to Eleazar and Ithamar, his remaining sons, "Take the grain offering that remains from the LORD's fire offerings, and eat it unleavened beside the altar, for it is most holy.

**13** You shall eat it in a holy place, because it is your due and your sons' due, from the LORD's fire offerings, for so I am commanded.

**14** But the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution you shall eat in a clean place, you and your sons and your daughters with you, for they are given as your due and your sons' due from the sacrifices of the peace offerings of the people of Israel.

**15** The thigh of the contribution and the breast of the wave offering they shall bring with the fire offerings of the fat, to wave as a wave offering before the LORD, and it shall be yours and your sons' with you as a due forever, as the LORD has commanded."

**16** Now Moses diligently inquired about the goat of the sin offering, and behold, it had been burned up. And he was angry with Eleazar and Ithamar, the surviving sons of Aaron, saying,

**17** "Why have you not eaten the sin offering in the place of the sanctuary, since it is a thing most holy and has been given to you that you may bear the iniquity of the congregation, to make atonement for them before the LORD?

**18** Behold, its blood was not brought into the inner part of the sanctuary. You certainly ought to have eaten it in the sanctuary, as I commanded."

**19** And Aaron said to Moses, "Behold, today they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering before the LORD, and yet such things as these have happened to me. If I had eaten the sin offering today, would it have been pleasing in the sight of the LORD?"

**20** And when Moses heard that, he approved.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 63r–63v.** Chapter 10 follows immediately on folio 63r after the close of chapter 9 (the inaugural theophanic fire). The parashah break before chapter 10 is, in most manuscript traditions, a setumah (closed section break), reflecting the tight narrative continuity between the theophanic fire that consumed the altar offerings (9:24) and the consuming fire that falls on Nadab and Abihu (10:2) — the same fire, now as instrument of judgment rather than approval. The chapter is structured around three scene-shifts: the death of Nadab and Abihu and the immediate aftermath (vv. 1–7); the divine word to Aaron regarding priestly sobriety and discernment (vv. 8–11); and the dispute over the uneaten sin offering goat (vv. 12–20). The verse count is twenty. The chapter closes with the only verse in Leviticus where Moses explicitly yields to Aaron's reasoning: va-yishma' Moshe vayyitav be-einav (v. 20 — "and Moses heard and it was good in his sight / he approved"). This is a theologically and narratively remarkable moment of Aaronic vindication and Mosaic deference.

The Tiberian vocalization is consistent throughout. Key orthographic features: the divine oracle of v. 3 is rendered as a direct quotation within Moses' speech — Davar asher dibber YHWH le'mor — and the quotation marks around the LORD's speech (vv. 3b) should be clearly demarcated. Verse 16's esh zarah opening without any narrative transition signals the jarring rupture of the sacred moment — the chapter begins mid-celebration and ends mid-crisis. The Hebrew verbal sequences of vv. 1–2 (vayyiqchu ... vayyitenu ... vayyasimu ... vayyaqrivu ... vattetse' esh... vattokal otam) form a tight wayyiqtol chain of rapid consecutive action, enacting the speed and finality of the judgment.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 94–95 (LXX).** The chapter continues immediately from the LXX's Leviticus 9 on p. 94. The LXX text of chapter 10 shows several significant divergences from the MT, most notably at v. 1 (the rendering of esh zarah), v. 3 (the wording of the divine oracle), v. 6 (the mourning prohibition addressed to Aaron), vv. 16–17 (the inquiry about the goat), and v. 20 (Aaron's vindication). The LXX at v. 16 reads notably differently: where the MT has Moses inquiring about the goat and being angry, the LXX's versional text shows some harmonizing tendencies. The uncial hand is legible throughout pp. 94–95 with no major lacunae evident in the facsimile.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"Unauthorized fire" / esh zarah (v. 1).** Hebrew אֵשׁ זָרָה (esh zarah — "strange fire / foreign fire / unauthorized fire"). The adjective זָרָה (zarah) is the feminine form of זָר (zar — "strange, foreign, unauthorized, belonging to another, alien"). The root zar designates that which is outside its proper boundary — the alien, the outsider, the one who does not belong to a particular sphere. In the cultic-priestly context of Leviticus, zar consistently designates the outsider to a priestly role or the unauthorized element in a priestly action: unauthorized persons offering incense (Num 16:40), unauthorized persons eating the holy portions (Lev 22:10), unauthorized priests performing priestly duties (Num 1:51). Here, esh zarah means fire that was not authorized — fire that YHWH had not commanded. The specific violation is debated in Jewish tradition: Did Nadab and Abihu take fire from a source other than the sacred altar fire established in 9:24 (i.e., common fire rather than divine fire)? Did they offer incense at an improper time? Did they enter the sanctuary in an altered state (hence the immediately following prohibition on priestly intoxication, vv. 8–11, which may be etiological)? Did they enter the inner sanctuary unbidden? The text does not specify; it only names the fire as zarah and adds the devastating clause asher lo' tsivvah otam — "which he had not commanded them." The criterion of divine command is the standard of priestly legitimacy throughout Leviticus. "Unauthorized fire" renders esh zarah with a term that captures the legal-priestly sense of the violation without over-specifying the nature of the unauthorized act. "Strange fire" (KJV, NKJV, ESV margin) is more literal. "Unauthorized fire" (NASB, ESV text) interprets zarah in its legal-cultic sense and is preferred for clarity. The LXX renders πῦρ ἀλλότριον ("alien/foreign fire"), using ἀλλότριος ("belonging to another, foreign, alien") for zarah — a close semantic match. LXX ἀλλότριον emphasizes the alien, outsider quality of the fire rather than its lack of divine authorization specifically, but the senses overlap significantly.

2. **"Before the LORD" / lifnei YHWH (vv. 1–2).** Hebrew לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (lifnei YHWH — "before the face of the LORD / in the presence of the LORD"). This spatial-relational phrase appears three times in vv. 1–2 (vv. 1, 2a, 2b): the fire is offered lifnei YHWH (v. 1); the divine fire goes out from before the LORD (vattetse' esh millifnei YHWH, v. 2a — "from before the LORD," using millifnei with the directional mem prefix); and they died lifnei YHWH (v. 2b). The triple occurrence of "before the LORD" creates an exact parallel with 9:24 (the theophanic fire that came out "from before the LORD" and was received with joy). In chapter 10, the fire moves in the reverse direction: unauthorized fire goes before the LORD (v. 1) and fire comes back out from before the LORD in judgment (v. 2). The spatial-theological symmetry is precise and ironic. "Before the LORD" renders lifnei YHWH throughout. The LXX renders ἔναντι κυρίου ("before/in the sight of the LORD"), using ἔναντι ("before, in the presence of, in the sight of") for lifnei — a standard LXX equivalence.

3. **"Consumed them" / vattokal otam (v. 2).** Hebrew וַתֹּאכַל אֹתָם (vattokal otam — "and it ate/consumed them"). The same verb אָכַל ('akhal — "to eat, to consume") is used at 9:24 for the fire that consumed (vattokal) the burnt offering and fat pieces. In chapter 10, the divine fire "eats" Nadab and Abihu in precisely the same language. The intertextual echo is unmistakable and theologically deliberate: the fire that consumed the authorized offerings in 9:24 is the same fire that consumes the unauthorized offerers in 10:2. This is not a different fire — it is the theophanic fire of YHWH's presence, which both accepts and judges. "Consumed them" renders vattokal otam with the same verb used in 9:24, allowing English readers to feel the verbal echo. The LXX renders κατέφαγεν αὐτούς ("devoured/consumed them"), using the intensive compound κατεσθίω — the same word used at 9:24 in the LXX, preserving the verbal echo precisely.

4. **"Among those who are near me I will be sanctified" / bi-qerovai eqqadesh (v. 3).** Hebrew בִּקְרֹבַי אֶקָּדֵשׁ (bi-qerovai eqqadesh — "among/by those near me I will be sanctified / by those who approach me I will show myself holy"). The verb אֶקָּדֵשׁ (eqqadesh) is the niphal imperfect of קדשׁ (qadash — "to be holy, to be treated as holy, to be sanctified"). The niphal here is reflexive-tolerative: "I will be sanctified" or "I will allow myself to be shown holy / I will sanctify myself." The subject of the verb is YHWH himself. Those who draw near to YHWH in the sanctuary — the priests, the inner circle, the qerovim — are precisely those who must honor his holiness most absolutely. The phrase qerovai ("those near me, my near ones") designates the priests whose priestly calling consists in drawing near to the divine presence (the root qrv is the same root as the sacrificial verb hiqqriv — "to bring near, to offer"). The statement of divine principle is paradoxical: the nearer one is to YHWH, the more one is exposed to the danger of his holiness being violated — and the more certainly YHWH will be shown holy, whether through obedience and blessing or through judgment. "Among those who are near me I will be sanctified" renders bi-qerovai eqqadesh with precise formal equivalence. The LXX renders ἐν τοῖς ἐγγίζουσίν μοι ἁγιασθήσομαι ("among/by those who draw near to me I will be sanctified"), using ἐγγίζω ("to draw near, to approach") for qerovai and ἁγιάζω (passive future) for eqqadesh — in close agreement.

5. **"And before all the people I will be glorified" / ve-al penei kol-ha-am ekkavedeh (v. 3).** Hebrew וְעַל-פְּנֵי כָל-הָעָם אֶכָּבֵד (ve-al-penei kol-ha-am ekkavedeh — "and before the face of all the people I will be glorified / I will be honored / I will display my weightiness"). The verb אֶכָּבֵד (ekkavedeh) is the niphal imperfect of כָּבַד (kavad — "to be heavy, to be weighty, to be honored/glorified"). The niphal: "to be honored, to be treated as weighty, to be glorified." This is the verbal form of kavod (glory). The divine declaration is a two-part parallelism: (a) I will be sanctified among those near me (the priests); (b) I will be glorified before all the people. The death of Nadab and Abihu is the means by which YHWH is publicly sanctified among the priests and glorified before the congregation. The kavod of YHWH (9:23) has now become the judgment that glorifies him through the public demonstration of his holiness. "And before all the people I will be glorified" renders ve-al-penei kol-ha-am ekkavedeh precisely. The LXX renders καὶ ἐν πάσῃ τῇ συναγωγῇ δοξασθήσομαι ("and among all the assembly I will be glorified"), using δοξάζω (passive future) for ekkavedeh and συναγωγή for ha-am — shifting "all the people" (kol-ha-am) to "all the assembly" (πάσῃ τῇ συναγωγῇ).

6. **"Aaron was silent" / vayyidom Aharon (v. 3).** Hebrew וַיִּדֹּם אַהֲרֹן (vayyidom Aharon — "and Aaron was silent / and Aaron kept silence"). The verb דָּמַם (damam) means to be silent, to be still, to be dumb with grief or stunned speechlessness. This is not the silence of assent but the silence of one who has been struck by incomprehensible grief and at the same time by an overpowering recognition of divine justice. Aaron's silence is one of the most profound moments in the entire Pentateuchal narrative. He has just seen his two eldest sons consumed by fire in the very moment of priestly inauguration. Moses quotes the divine oracle (v. 3); Aaron is silent. Rabbinic tradition (Sifra; Rashi on v. 3) treats Aaron's silence as the highest form of acceptance of divine justice — vayyidom, "he was still" or "he was silenced," as a model of theodicy. The verb damam appears also in Ps 4:4 ("be still in your hearts"), Lam 3:26, and especially Ps 37:7 ("be still before the LORD"). "Aaron was silent" renders vayyidom Aharon precisely and without elaboration, allowing the silence to speak. The LXX renders καὶ κατενύχθη Ααρων ("and Aaron was pierced/smitten / was struck dumb"), using κατανύσσω ("to pierce, to prick, to strike with compunction/grief") — a significantly more emotionally interpretive rendering. The LXX's κατενύχθη suggests Aaron was inwardly pierced with grief and compunction (the same root appears in Acts 2:37 — "they were cut to the heart"). The MT's vayyidom captures an exterior silence; the LXX's κατενύχθη captures an interior wounding. Both are theologically resonant, but the MT's restraint is the original.

7. **"Do not let your hair hang loose" / roshekhem al tifra'u (v. 6).** Hebrew רָאשֵׁיכֶם אַל-תִּפְרָעוּ (roshekhem al-tifra'u — "your heads do not loosen / do not let your hair hang loose"). The verb פָּרַע (para') means to uncover, to let go, to let hang loose. In the context of mourning practices, the loosening or disheveling of the hair was a conventional sign of mourning (cf. Lev 13:45, where the leper must let his hair hang loose as a sign of defilement and isolation; Num 5:18, the accused wife with loosened hair). For the officiating priests, the mourning gestures of loose hair and torn garments are explicitly prohibited. The high priest is forbidden from mourning rites entirely (Lev 21:10–12): he must not uncover his head or tear his clothes even for a father or mother. The prohibition here extends that restriction to Aaron and his remaining sons during the tabernacle service. "Do not let your hair hang loose" renders roshekhem al-tifra'u with the standard formal rendering (ESV). The LXX renders τὰς κεφαλὰς ὑμῶν οὐκ ἀποκαλύψετε ("you shall not uncover your heads"), using ἀποκαλύπτω ("to uncover, to reveal") for para' — capturing the uncovering aspect of the gesture.

8. **"Do not tear your garments" / u-vigdekhem lo tifromu (v. 6).** Hebrew וּבִגְדֵיכֶם לֹא תִפְרֹמוּ (u-vigdekhem lo tifromu — "and your garments you shall not tear"). The verb פָּרַם (param) means to tear, to rend — specifically a garment. The tearing of garments (qriah) was the standard Near Eastern and Israelite gesture of grief and mourning (cf. Gen 37:29, 34; 44:13; Josh 7:6; 2 Sam 1:11). In priestly mourning law (Lev 21:10), the high priest is forbidden from tearing his garments (bigadav lo' yifrom). The identical prohibition here for Aaron and his sons during the sanctuary service reflects the same principle: the priestly vestments are consecrated, part of the sacral complex — to tear them during the very service in which YHWH has just manifested his glory would be a desecration of the sanctuary. "Do not tear your garments" renders u-vigdekhem lo tifromu precisely. The LXX renders τὰ ἱμάτια ὑμῶν οὐ διαρρήξετε ("you shall not tear your garments"), using διαρρήγνυμι ("to tear apart, to rend") — in close agreement.

9. **"Lest you die, and wrath come upon all the congregation" / pen tamuthu ve-al kol-ha-edah yiqtsof (v. 6).** Hebrew פֶּן-תָּמֻתוּ וְעַל כָּל-הָעֵדָה יִקְצֹף (pen tamuthu ve-al kol-ha-edah yiqtsof — "lest you die and upon all the congregation wrath break out"). The verb קָצַף (qatsaf) means to be angry, to be provoked to wrath, to break out in fury — used of YHWH's wrath in particular (Lev 10:6; Num 16:46; Deut 1:34). The phrase "wrath come upon all the congregation" indicates that the death of Nadab and Abihu and the crisis of chapter 10 are not merely a priestly-family matter: if Aaron and his sons abandon their stations through mourning, the entire congregation stands exposed to YHWH's wrath. The priests' ongoing service is a protective function for the people. "Wrath come upon all the congregation" renders ve-al kol-ha-edah yiqtsof precisely. The LXX renders καὶ ἐπὶ πᾶσαν τὴν συναγωγὴν θυμωθήσεται ("and against all the assembly he will be angry/wrathful"), using θυμόω (passive, "to be filled with anger") for qatsaf — in close agreement.

10. **"The burning that the LORD has kindled" / ha-sreifah asher saraf YHWH (v. 6).** Hebrew הַשְּׂרֵפָה אֲשֶׁר שָׂרַף יְהוָה (ha-sreifah asher saraf YHWH — "the burning that the LORD has burned / that the LORD burned"). The noun שְׂרֵפָה (sreifah — "burning, conflagration") and the cognate verb שָׂרַף (saraf — "to burn") combine in a figura etymologica: "the burning that he burned." "The burning that the LORD has kindled" renders ha-sreifah asher saraf YHWH with a natural English idiom that captures the violent consuming fire without the awkward repetition of "burning he burned." The LXX renders τὸ κάταμμα ὃ κατεκάη κύριος ("the burning / conflagration which the LORD burned/kindled") — preserving the figura etymologica more explicitly with κάταμμα (burning, combustion) and κατακαίω (to burn down, to consume by fire). The LXX's choice of κάταμμα is unusual; the more common LXX term for sreifah is κατακαυμός.

11. **"You are to distinguish between the holy and the common" / u-lehavdil bein ha-qodesh u-vein ha-chol (v. 10).** Hebrew וּלְהַבְדִּיל בֵּין הַקֹּדֶשׁ וּבֵין הַחֹל (u-lehavdil bein ha-qodesh u-vein ha-chol — "and to separate/distinguish between the holy and the common/profane"). The verb הַבְדִּיל (havdil, hifil infinitive of bdl — "to separate, to distinguish, to divide") is the key verb of priestly discernment. The same verb is used of God's creative separations in Genesis 1 (God separated light from darkness, waters above from waters below); it is the verb of the Havdalah ceremony marking the separation of Shabbat from the weekday. In the priestly context, havdil is the fundamental cognitive and practical act that defines the priestly vocation: to distinguish between the sacred (qodesh) and the common or non-sacred (chol). The two binary axes of v. 10 — holy/common and unclean/clean (tameh/tahor) — are the twin grids of the entire Levitical purity and holiness system. The immediately preceding narrative (Nadab and Abihu, vv. 1–2) is itself an instance of failure to make the havdil distinction: unauthorized fire (esh zarah) is common/alien fire brought into a holy space and holy function. The priestly sobriety statute of vv. 8–9 is thus directly linked to the havdil duty: a priest who is impaired cannot perform the discernment function. "You are to distinguish between the holy and the common" renders u-lehavdil bein ha-qodesh u-vein ha-chol with precise formal equivalence. The LXX renders διαστεῖλαι ἀνὰ μέσον τῶν ἁγίων καὶ τῶν βεβήλων ("to distinguish between the holy things and the profane/common things"), using διαστέλλω ("to separate, to distinguish, to command distinctly") and βέβηλος ("profane, common, not sacred") for chol — in close agreement.

12. **"Strong drink" / shekhar (v. 9).** Hebrew שֵׁכָר (shekhar — "strong drink / intoxicating drink"). The term shekhar designates fermented beverages other than grape wine (yayin) — including date beer, barley beer, and other grain or fruit fermentations. It is consistently paired with wine in prohibition or praise contexts: the Nazirite vow forbids both yayin and shekhar (Num 6:3); Proverbs commends shekhar to those who are perishing (Prov 31:6); Isaiah condemns priests who err through shekhar (Isa 28:7 — a possible echo of the Nadab and Abihu incident). The pairing of yayin ve-shekhar ("wine and strong drink") in v. 9 creates an inclusive merism: no fermented beverage of any type, whether grape wine or grain/fruit ferments. "Strong drink" renders shekhar as the standard formal translation (ESV, KJV, NASB). The LXX renders σικερα, a direct Greek transliteration of shekhar rather than a translation — demonstrating that the LXX translators either could not find an adequate Greek equivalent or recognized shekhar as a technical term requiring transliteration. σικερα passed into Greek and thence into Latin (sicera) as a loanword.

13. **"Most holy" / qodesh qodashim (v. 12).** Hebrew קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qodashim — "holiness of holinesses / holy of holies / most holy"). The superlative construction in Hebrew uses a genitive of the same noun: the holiest of holy things. The qodesh qodashim designation (applied to the sin offering, grain offering, and guilt offering portions that belong to the priests in Lev 6–7) indicates the highest category of sanctity in the Levitical holiness taxonomy. These portions must be eaten in the sanctuary (not at home), by male priests only, within the prescribed time. Crucially, it is precisely the qodesh qodashim status of the sin offering goat that is at stake in vv. 16–20: Moses is angry because the most holy sin offering was burned rather than eaten in the sanctuary. "Most holy" renders qodesh qodashim with the standard English superlative. The LXX renders ἅγια ἁγίων ("holy of holies / holiest of holies / most holy things"), in direct agreement with the superlative construction.

14. **"Bear the iniquity of the congregation" / la-set et-avon ha-edah (v. 17).** Hebrew לָשֵׂאת אֶת-עֲוֹן הָעֵדָה (la-set et-avon ha-edah — "to carry / to bear the iniquity of the congregation"). The verb נָשָׂא (nasa') in the infinitive construct (la-set) means "to lift up, to carry, to bear." The object is עָוֹן ('avon — "iniquity, guilt, the consequence of iniquity"). The phrase nasa' 'avon — "to bear iniquity" — is a technical expression in the Levitical sacrifice system: the priest, by eating the sin offering, symbolically takes the iniquity of the offerer upon himself, and through the atoning mechanism of the sacrifice, removes it. The priest as bearer of iniquity is the precise theological function of the sin offering priestly portion. Moses' accusation in v. 17 is therefore not merely procedural (the rules were not followed) but theological: by not eating the sin offering, Eleazar and Ithamar failed to perform their iniquity-bearing function. "That you may bear the iniquity of the congregation" renders la-set et-avon ha-edah precisely. The LXX renders ὥστε ἀφελεῖν τὴν ἁμαρτίαν τῆς συναγωγῆς ("so as to take away/remove the sin of the assembly"), using ἀφαιρέω ("to take away, to remove") rather than a bearing/carrying verb — interpreting the nasa' in its consequential effect (removal) rather than its literal action (bearing). This is a meaningful LXX interpretive departure.

15. **"Its blood was not brought into the inner part of the sanctuary" / lo-huva et-damah el-qerev ha-qodesh panimah (v. 18).** Hebrew לֹא-הוּבָא אֶת-דָּמָהּ אֶל-קֶרֶב הַקֹּדֶשׁ פְּנִימָה (lo-huva et-damah el-qerev ha-qodesh panimah — "its blood was not brought into the inner part of the sanctuary / into the inner part of the holy place within"). The phrase qerev ha-qodesh panimah designates the inner sanctuary — the Holy of Holies or the inner room of the tabernacle, as distinct from the outer court. Moses' argument in v. 18 rests on the rule established in Lev 6:30: if the blood of a sin offering is brought into the tent of meeting (inner sanctuary) for atonement, the flesh must not be eaten but must be burned (as with the priest's own sin offering of Lev 4:3–21; the congregation's sin offering of Lev 4:13–21). If, however, the blood is applied only at the outer altar (as with the leader's or individual's sin offering of Lev 4:22–35), the flesh must be eaten by the priests in the sanctuary. In chapter 10, the sin offering goat is the people's sin offering of the inaugural day (9:15) — its blood was applied to the outer altar horns (not brought into the inner sanctuary). Therefore, Moses argues, the rule of Lev 6:26, 29 applies: the priests should have eaten it. "Its blood was not brought into the inner part of the sanctuary" renders lo-huva et-damah el-qerev ha-qodesh panimah precisely. The LXX renders οὐ γὰρ εἰσηνέχθη τὸ αἷμα αὐτῆς εἰς τὸ ἅγιον ("for its blood was not brought into the holy place"), using εἰσφέρω ("to bring in") and τὸ ἅγιον ("the holy place / the sanctuary") — a somewhat condensed rendering that omits panimah ("within/inward") from the MT's full locative phrase.

16. **"He approved" / vayyitav be-einav (v. 20).** Hebrew וַיִּיטַב בְּעֵינָיו (vayyitav be-einav — "and it was good in his eyes / and it pleased him"). The idiom tov be-einei + subject means "it was good/pleasing in the eyes of" someone — a common Hebrew expression for approval, agreement, or satisfaction. The subject of "it was good" is Moses — "and it was good in Moses' eyes" or "it pleased Moses." Moses hears Aaron's defense (v. 19) and the text simply notes: vayyitav be-einav. This is the closing word of the chapter and one of the most concise and powerful moments in Leviticus. Moses is satisfied. Aaron is vindicated. The crisis is resolved without punishment. The chapter that began with death ends with approval. "He approved" is an idiomatic and somewhat understated rendering of vayyitav be-einav — choosing to bring out the evaluative and interpersonal sense of the idiom rather than the literal optical metaphor ("it was good in his eyes"). The LXX renders καὶ ἤρεσεν Μωυσεῖ ("and it pleased Moses"), using ἀρέσκω ("to please, to be satisfactory to") — functionally equivalent and similarly concise, with Moses made the direct dative object ("pleased Moses") rather than using the eye-idiom.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants (pp. 94–95) — Significant Divergences from MT

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10:1 | esh zarah ("unauthorized fire / strange fire") | πῦρ ἀλλότριον ("alien/foreign fire") | LXX ἀλλότριος ("belonging to another, alien") for MT zarah ("strange, foreign, unauthorized"). Semantic equivalence; LXX foregrounds the alien/outsider quality of the fire. |
| 10:2 | vattokal otam ("and it consumed them") | κατέφαγεν αὐτούς ("devoured them") | Same intensive compound κατεσθίω used in LXX 9:24 for the fire consuming the offerings — exact verbal echo preserved in LXX as in MT. |
| 10:3 | vayyidom Aharon ("and Aaron was silent") | καὶ κατενύχθη Ααρων ("and Aaron was pierced with grief / was struck dumb") | Significant LXX interpretive expansion. MT's vayyidom (silence, stillness) captures exterior composure; LXX's κατενύχθη (from κατανύσσω — to pierce, to prick to compunction) suggests interior grief and compunction. LXX adds emotional depth not explicit in the MT. |
| 10:3 | bi-qerovai eqqadesh / ve-al-penei kol-ha-am ekkavedeh | ἐν τοῖς ἐγγίζουσίν μοι ἁγιασθήσομαι / καὶ ἐν πάσῃ τῇ συναγωγῇ δοξασθήσομαι | LXX renders kol-ha-am ("all the people") as πάσῃ τῇ συναγωγῇ ("all the assembly"), using συναγωγή for ha-am in a stylistic adjustment. Otherwise close agreement. |
| 10:6 | roshekhem al-tifra'u ("do not let your hair hang loose") | τὰς κεφαλὰς ὑμῶν οὐκ ἀποκαλύψετε ("you shall not uncover your heads") | MT para' = to loosen/dishevel (hair); LXX ἀποκαλύπτω = to uncover. The MT specifies the mourning hair-gesture (disheveling); the LXX generalizes to uncovering the head. Slight semantic shift from disheveled hair to uncovered head. |
| 10:6 | ha-sreifah asher saraf YHWH ("the burning that the LORD has kindled") | τὸ κάταμμα ὃ κατεκάη κύριος ("the burning which the LORD burned") | LXX preserves the MT's figura etymologica (burning/burned). Unusual LXX noun κάταμμα for sreifah; more common LXX term would be κατακαυμός. |
| 10:9 | shekhar ("strong drink") | σικερα (transliteration) | LXX does not translate shekhar but transliterates it as σικερα — a loanword that passed into Greek, Latin (sicera), and ultimately English etymological tradition. Signals the LXX translators' recognition of shekhar as a culture-specific technical term. |
| 10:10 | bein ha-qodesh u-vein ha-chol ("between the holy and the common") | ἀνὰ μέσον τῶν ἁγίων καὶ τῶν βεβήλων ("between the holy things and the profane things") | LXX βέβηλος ("profane, desecrated, unhallowed") for MT chol ("common, ordinary, non-sacred"). LXX βέβηλος carries a stronger connotation of desecration than MT chol; chol is morally neutral ("ordinary"), while βέβηλος suggests something that has crossed the holy threshold improperly. Slight LXX intensification. |
| 10:12 | qodesh qodashim ("most holy") | ἅγια ἁγίων ("holiest of holies / most holy") | Standard agreement on the superlative construction. |
| 10:16 | darosh darash Moshe ("Moses diligently inquired") | ζητῶν ἐξεζήτησεν Μωυσῆς ("Moses carefully sought/inquired") | Both MT and LXX preserve the cognate intensification (darosh darash = figura etymologica; ζητῶν ἐξεζήτησεν = participle + compound verb intensification). Close agreement in emphasis. |
| 10:17 | la-set et-avon ha-edah ("to bear the iniquity of the congregation") | ὥστε ἀφελεῖν τὴν ἁμαρτίαν τῆς συναγωγῆς ("so as to take away/remove the sin of the assembly") | Significant LXX interpretive departure. MT nasa' ("to bear, to carry") is rendered by LXX ἀφαιρέω ("to take away, to remove") — shifting from the sacrificial bearing/carrying metaphor to the result of removal. LXX also uses ἁμαρτία ("sin") for MT 'avon ("iniquity, guilt"), and συναγωγή for edah. |
| 10:18 | el-qerev ha-qodesh panimah ("into the inner part of the sanctuary") | εἰς τὸ ἅγιον ("into the holy place") | LXX condenses the MT's full locative phrase (qerev ha-qodesh panimah — "into the innermost part of the sanctuary, within") to simply εἰς τὸ ἅγιον ("into the holy place"). The MT's double locative (qerev + panimah — "inner + inward") specifying the innermost chamber is partially lost in the LXX's simpler reading. |
| 10:19 | ve-tiqre'nah oti ka-eleh ("and such things as these have happened to me") | καί μοι συνέβη ταῦτα ("and these things have happened to me") | Close agreement. LXX συμβαίνω ("to happen, to befall, to come together") for MT qara' ("to happen, to befall, to meet, to encounter"). Both render Aaron's speech as a description of calamity that has befallen him. |
| 10:20 | vayyitav be-einav ("and it was good in his eyes / he approved") | καὶ ἤρεσεν Μωυσεῖ ("and it pleased Moses") | MT uses the eye-idiom (tov be-einei — "good in the eyes of"); LXX uses ἀρέσκω ("to please") with Moses as dative object. Functional equivalence with different idiomatic structures. Both preserve the concise, unelaborated approval as the chapter's closing note. |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings (Leviticus Series — Updated with Chapter 10)

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| אֵשׁ זָרָה (esh zarah) | unauthorized fire | Fire not divinely commanded; LXX: πῦρ ἀλλότριον ("alien fire") |
| לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (lifnei YHWH) | before the LORD | Spatial-relational presence idiom; LXX: ἔναντι κυρίου |
| בִּקְרֹבַי (bi-qerovai) | among those who are near me | Priestly inner circle; LXX: ἐν τοῖς ἐγγίζουσίν μοι |
| אֶקָּדֵשׁ (eqqadesh, niphal) | I will be sanctified | Divine passive — YHWH shows himself holy; LXX: ἁγιασθήσομαι |
| אֶכָּבֵד (ekkavedeh, niphal) | I will be glorified | Verbal form of kavod — divine self-display in weight/glory; LXX: δοξασθήσομαι |
| וַיִּדֹּם (vayyidom) | was silent | Speechless grief and/or awe; LXX: κατενύχθη ("was pierced with compunction") |
| פָּרַע (para') | hang loose (hair) | Mourning dishevelment gesture; LXX: ἀποκαλύπτω ("uncover") |
| פָּרַם (param) | tear (garments) | Mourning rending gesture; LXX: διαρρήγνυμι |
| קָצַף (qatsaf) | wrath | Divine anger / wrath-breaking-out; LXX: θυμόω |
| הַבְדִּיל (havdil, hifil) | distinguish | Priestly discernment verb; LXX: διαστέλλω |
| קֹדֶשׁ (qodesh) | holy | Sacred, set apart; LXX: ἅγιος |
| חֹל (chol) | common | Non-sacred; LXX: βέβηλος ("profane") |
| טָמֵא (tameh) | unclean | Ritually defiled; LXX: ἀκάθαρτος |
| טָהוֹר (tahor) | clean | Ritually pure; LXX: καθαρός |
| שֵׁכָר (shekhar) | strong drink | Non-grape ferment; LXX: σικερα (transliteration) |
| קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qodashim) | most holy | Superlative of holiness; LXX: ἅγια ἁγίων |
| לָשֵׂאת עָוֹן (la-set 'avon) | bear the iniquity | Priestly sin-absorption function; LXX: ἀφελεῖν τὴν ἁμαρτίαν ("remove the sin") |
| וַיִּיטַב בְּעֵינָיו (vayyitav be-einav) | he approved | Eye-idiom for satisfaction/approval; LXX: ἤρεσεν ("it pleased") |
| חַטָּאת (chattat) | sin offering | Established from Lev 4; LXX: ἁμαρτία / περὶ ἁμαρτίας |
| עֹלָה (olah) | burnt offering | Established from Lev 1; LXX: ὁλοκαύτωμα |
| שְׁלָמִים (shelamim) | peace offerings | Established from Lev 3; LXX: σωτήριον |
| מִנְחָה (minchah) | grain offering | Established from Lev 2; LXX: θυσία |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ohel mo'ed) | tent of meeting | LXX: σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου |
| יהוה | the LORD | YHWH — consistent throughout |
| מֹשֶׁה (Moshe) | Moses | English name form |
| אַהֲרֹן (Aharon) | Aaron | English name form |
| יִשְׂרָאֵל (Yisrael) | Israel | English name form |
| אֵלְעָזָר (El'azar) | Eleazar | English name form |
| אִיתָמָר (Itamar) | Ithamar | English name form |
| נָדָב (Nadav) | Nadab | English name form |
| אֲבִיהוּא (Avihu) | Abihu | English name form |

— LOGOS
